title: Connecting a shower tray waste
subtitle: Fit and connect the waste outlet and trap on a new shower tray
excerpt: How to fit a shower tray waste outlet and trap to a new acrylic or stone resin shower tray, connect the trap to the soil stack or existing waste pipe, seal the waste plate to the tray, and test for leaks before the shower enclosure panels are fitted.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Plumbworld installation guides. Building Regulations Part H (2002) drainage and waste disposal.

glossaryTerms.definition: A water-seal trap fitted below the shower tray waste outlet. The trapped water prevents sewer gas entering the room. Shower traps are available in standard (75 mm) and low-profile (50 mm) formats; the low-profile version is used where floor-to-waste clearance is limited. All must carry a minimum 20 mm water seal to comply with Building Regulations Part H.

An adhesive for permanently joining ABS or PVC waste fittings. The solvent partially dissolves both surfaces; as it evaporates, the surfaces fuse. Joints are permanent and should not be worked once the cement begins to grab (30 to 60 seconds). The pipe socket must be dry and degreased before cementing.

A pre-formed aperture in a soil pipe or waste stack into which a new branch connection is inserted. Shower waste pipes must enter the soil stack or a 40 mm waste branch — they cannot discharge directly to an open gully without a trap. The stack boss is sealed with a rubber compression ring.

content.text: The shower waste connection must be made before the shower tray is set on its mortar bed or feet and before any wall panels are fixed. Once the tray is in its final position with panels fitted, access to the trap and pipe run is difficult or impossible without major disruption. Dry-fit everything first to confirm the trap will connect to the existing waste pipe at the correct angle and fall.
Step 1, fit the waste outlet to the tray

Place the tray upside down on a padded surface. Apply a continuous bead of sanitary-grade silicone sealant around the underside of the waste outlet flange. Press the outlet into the tray aperture from above and fit the rubber washer and locking ring to the underside, threading the supplied screws through the outlet plate from above. Tighten the screws evenly: do not overtighten, as this distorts the outlet flange and breaks the silicone seal.

onto the waste outlet thread. Do not use PTFE tape on the trap-to-outlet thread: the joint uses a rubber washer, not a threaded seal. Hand-tighten, then a further quarter turn with the adjustable spanner. Confirm the trap outlet faces in the direction of the waste pipe run.
Step 3, connect to the waste pipe

Cut the 40 mm waste pipe to length using the pipe cutter. The waste pipe must fall at a minimum of 18 mm per metre (1 in 55) toward the stack or drain: check the fall with the spirit level. Connect the pipe to the trap using push-fit or solvent-welded fittings. For a push-fit connection, ensure the pipe end is cut square, deburred, and marked with a depth gauge mark. For
fittings, clean the pipe end and socket interior with the manufacturer's cleaning fluid before applying cement to both surfaces.
Step 4, connect to the stack or branch
Connect the waste run to an existing 40 mm waste branch or to a

in the soil stack. A boss-entry fitting requires drilling through the soil stack wall with a hole saw, inserting the rubber sleeve of the boss connector, and clamping with the retaining ring. Ensure the connector is inserted at the same direction as the water flow in the stack: a fitting angled against the flow causes splashback.
Step 5, test before closing the tray

Set the tray in its final position on its feet or mortar bed. Pour several litres of water from a bucket into the waste outlet and watch the trap and waste pipe run for any drips. Check the trap joint, the pipe-to-trap joint, and the stack connection. Confirm the water drains away cleanly. Only then fit the shower enclosure panels and seal the tray perimeter with sanitary silicone.

Linear shower trays use a channel drain rather than a round waste outlet. The channel body is installed into the tray slot and connected to a 40 mm or 50 mm waste pipe below. The fall on a linear-drain tray is designed into the tray surface itself: the tray must be installed level in order for the built-in fall to work correctly.

attrs.cause: Insufficient fall on the waste pipe; partial blockage in the trap or pipe.
attrs.fix: Check the fall with a spirit level along the full waste run — minimum 18 mm per metre. Clear the trap and test flow. If still slow, rod the pipe with a drain snake.

attrs.cause: Trap water seal has evaporated (if the shower has not been used for a period); trap seal less than 20 mm.
attrs.fix: Pour a jug of water into the waste to restore the seal. If the smell returns when the shower is in regular use, the trap does not comply with Part H — replace with a deeper-seal trap.

attrs.symptom: Leak at the waste outlet-to-tray joint
attrs.cause: Silicone sealant applied to a wet or greasy surface; outlet screws overtightened and distorted the outlet flange.
attrs.fix: Remove the outlet, clean the tray aperture, allow to dry completely, and refit with fresh sealant on a clean dry surface.
